Original page 5
Vorbemerkungen  -  Preliminary remarks
This manual applies to the chassis of the 7.5 cm Sturmgeschütz 40, models F/8 and G, and of the 10.5 cm Sturmhaubitze 42, model G. The F/8 and G chassis agrees in all important components with the chassis of the Panzerkampfwagen III (Pz.Kpfw. III), model J.
Associated manuals (Dazugehörige Vorschriften):
D 652/43b - 7.5 cm Sturmgeschütz 40 (models F, F/8 and G) and 10.5 cm Sturmhaubitze 42 (model G): equipment description and handling instructions for the superstructure (Aufbau).
D 652/44 - schedule booklet (Fristenheft) for lubrication and maintenance work.
D 652/47 - maintenance booklet (Pflegeheft).
Additionally, as an alternative (Ferner wahlweise):
D 218 - The 7.5 cm assault gun (Sturmkanone) 40, description; or
D 2002 - The 10.5 cm Sturmhaubitze 42, description.

Original page 7
A. Technische Angaben  -  A. Technical data

Original page 8
B. Beschreibung  -  B. Description
For the correct handling of the assault gun, and for the faster detection and elimination of faults, exact knowledge of the individual parts and their interaction is required.
Only careful handling and correct maintenance of the equipment guarantee good performance and protect against premature deterioration (becoming unserviceable).
Special occurrences that impair the serviceability of the equipment are to be reported immediately through the prescribed channel.
1. Allgemeines  -  1. General
The assault gun consists of the chassis (Fahrgestell) and the armoured superstructure (Panzeraufbau) mounted on it.
The chassis and superstructure are separated by the parting joint (Trennfuge).
This manual covers only the chassis. The superstructure is described in D 652/43b.
2. Hauptteile des Fahrgestells  -  2. Main components of the chassis
Bild 1-2
The chassis consists of the following main components:
armoured hull (Panzerwanne)
engine with cooling system and accessories (Motor mit Kühlanlage und Zubehör)
main clutch (Hauptkupplung)
cardan shaft (Gelenkwelle)
gearbox with blower (Wechselgetriebe mit Gebläse)
bevel drive and steering gear with support brakes and cardan shafts (Kegeltrieb, Lenkgetriebe mit Stützbremsen und Gelenkwellen)
steering brake (Lenkbremse)
final drive (Seitenvorgelege)
running gear (Laufwerk)
track (Gleiskette)
steering system (Lenkanlage)
electrical equipment (Elektrische Ausrüstung)
switchboard and bulkhead (Schaltbrett und Zwischenwand)
tools (Werkzeug)
auxiliary equipment (Hilfsgerät)
entrenching tools (Schanzzeug)

Original page 9
The armoured hull (Panzerwanne) is built as the load-bearing member (Träger) of the chassis; the engine and transmission assembly (Motor- und Getriebeanlage) is mounted in it.
The engine, with the main clutch (Hauptkupplung) flanged to it, is housed in the rear compartment (Heckraum) of the hull. On both sides of the engine are the radiators (Kühler) with the fans (Lüfter); and on the right-hand side of the vehicle, to the right of the engine and shielded from the engine heat by a wall, a fuel tank (Kraftstoffbehälter) is arranged.
A partition wall (Trennwand), provided with openings for the power transmission, the linkages (Gestänge) and the electrical leads, separates the engine from the fighting compartment (Kampfraum).
The cardan shaft (Gelenkwelle) runs from the main clutch flanged to the engine, through a tunnel through the crew compartment (Mannschaftsraum) and into the bow compartment (Bugraum) to the gearbox (Wechselgetriebe). At the front, the bevel drive (Kegeltrieb) with the steering gears (Lenkgetriebe) is flanged to the gearbox.
From the steering gears, a side shaft (Seitenwelle) runs left and right to the two steering brakes (Lenkbremsen) and the two final drives (Seitenvorgelege). The steering-brake mountings are flanged to the inside of the hull side walls, the final drives — which carry the drive sprockets (Triebräder) — to the outside.
To the left, next to the gearbox, is the driver's seat (Fahrersitz). In front of it are the accelerator, brake and clutch pedals (Fahr-, Brems- und Kupplungsfußhebel); on either side of the seat are the steering levers (Lenkhebel).
At the rear, the idler wheels (Leiträder) are carried on adjustable crank axles (Kurbelachsen). Between the drive sprocket and the idler wheel, each side of the vehicle has six road wheels (Laufrollen) sprung by torsion bars (Stabfedern). Above the road wheels, three return rollers (Stützrollen) are provided on each side.
The tracks (Gleisketten) are tensioned over all the wheels and are driven by the drive sprockets (Triebräder).
3. Panzerwanne  -  Armoured hull
The hull consists of the closed bow armour (Bugpanzer), the open centre section (Wannenmittelteil) and the open rear compartment (Heckraum), formed from several welded-together armour plates (Panzerbleche) of various thicknesses.
The side walls are braced against each other in the middle by a transverse wall (Querwand). The rear wall (Heckwand) is likewise reinforced to carry the idler-wheel axles (Leitradachsen).
At the top edge there is a flange (Flansch) onto which the armoured superstructure (Panzeraufbau) is bolted.
The floor (Boden) is stiffened transversely and longitudinally by bearers (Träger), which at the same time serve to secure the drive components (Antriebsteile).

Original page 12
(continued from page 11)
… pumps and forced into the reservoir (Vorratsbehälter), from which a gear pump (Zahnradpumpe) delivers it, via an oil cooler (Ölkühler) and an oil filter, to the engine's lubrication points (Schmierstellen).
The oil filter is designed as a gap (edge-type) filter (Spaltfilter). It holds a pack of thin metal discs (Metallplättchen) strung on a rotatable spindle (Spindel) so that they leave a narrow gap between them.
The oil flows through the disc pack from outside to inside, so the entrained dirt is deposited in front of the gaps. Narrow metal tongues (Metallzungen) can be turned with the spindle through the gaps between the discs; this scrapes off the dirt, which collects below in a removable bowl (Glocke). The spindle is worked by a linkage (Gestänge) connected to the clutch pedal (Kupplungsfußhebel).
b) Kühlanlage, Bild 3  -  Cooling system, Figure 3
A water pump (Wasserpumpe) circulates the cooling water. It sits on top of the crankcase and is driven from the crankshaft by two V-belts, together with the dynamo.
On each side of the engine there are two water radiators (Wasserkühler) whose upper header tanks (Wasserkästen) are joined by a balance pipe (Ausgleichrohr). This ensures that, in every inclined attitude of the vehicle, the engine and radiators always remain filled with water.
The cooling system is sized to be sufficient at an outside temperature of 30 °C. Vehicles of the tropical version (Tropenausführung) have a more generously sized system, giving adequate cooling up to an outside temperature of 42 °C. A spring-loaded relief valve (Überdruckventil) on the header tank of the left radiator is set so that, at peak engine load, it can allow a temperature of 105 °C in the cooling system (corresponding to an overpressure of about 0.3 kg/cm²). Steam blowing off this valve is led out of the hull through a pipe.
Behind each radiator is a fan (Lüfter) delivering the air needed for cooling. Air is drawn in through the side openings of the rear armour (Heckpanzer), passes through the radiators and is forced out through the rear openings. Special air flaps (Luftklappen) are fitted behind the cooling-air inlets; depending on the outside temperature they can be opened more or less from the fighting compartment by a hand lever.

Original page 13
The fan drive (Lüfterantrieb) is taken from the engine crankshaft via a cardan shaft (Gelenkwelle) to a belt pulley (Riemenscheibe) and thence by V-belts. The rear pulley is carried on a journal (Zapfen) whose height is adjustable by a double eccentric (Doppelexzenter) so that the V-belts can be tensioned. The eccentric is adjusted through an unscrewable cover in the rear wall.
To protect the belts against overload, a slip clutch (Rutschkupplung) is fitted between the fan-drive pulley (Lüfterantriebsscheibe) and the fan shaft (Lüfterwelle).
c) Vergaser und Luftfilter, Bild 4  -  Carburettors and air filter, Figure 4
Two Solex two-stage downdraught cross-country carburettors (Geländevergaser) are fitted on the engine, each serving the intake manifold (Ansaugleitung) of six cylinders.
Each cross-country carburettor is made up of two individual carburettors, the second of which only comes into action when the accelerator pedal (Gasfußhebel) is pressed well down.
So that the engine gets the correct mixture in all inclined attitudes, each individual carburettor has two floats (Schwimmer) acting on a centrally arranged jet (Düse). They act through a common shaft which, on the level, is turned by both floats; when the vehicle tilts sideways, only the lower float moves the shaft and keeps the correct fuel level at the jet.
The starting device (Anlaßvorrichtung), housed together with the main carburettor (Hauptvergaser), lets the engine start well even when cold. It is worked via a linkage by a starting lever (Anlaßhebel) to the left of the gearbox. Note that the throttle butterfly (Drosselklappe) of the main carburettor must stay closed — i.e. the accelerator pedal (Fahrfußhebel) must not be pressed while doing this.
The combustion air the engine needs is cleaned in four air filters (Luftfilter).
The air filters — Mahle oil-bath vortex filters (Mahle Wirbelölfilter) — sit in the engine compartment above the engine and connect to the carburettor through a forked pipe (Gabelrohr).
Through an intake elbow (Ansaugkrümmer) fitted tangentially to the filter housing, the engine draws in the air and sets the oil bath (Ölbad) in the housing into a swirling motion. The dust in the intake air is flung against the outer wall of the housing and washed by the oil into the lower sludge chamber (Schlammraum). The air pre-cleaned in this way …
(continued on page 14)

Original page 14
(continued from page 13)
… then passes through a fine filter (Feinfilter) and is led on through the outlet stub (Auslaßstutzen) and the forked pipe to the carburettor.
d) Anlasser  -  Starter
An electric starter (elektrischer Anlasser) and an inertia starter (Schwungkraftanlasser) are provided for starting the engine.
The electric starter, on the right of the engine, is worked by a push-button (Druckknopf) on the instrument panel (Schaltbrett).
The inertia starter is on the left of the engine and is connected by a cardan shaft to a cranking claw (Andrehklaue) mounted at the rear. Turning an insertable hand crank (Andrehkurbel) stores a large amount of energy in this starter's flywheel mass (Schwungmasse), with which the engine is turned over.
The starter pinion (Anlaßritzel) is engaged with the flywheel teeth (Schwungrad) by a handle (Handgriff) linked by a rod to an engagement lever (Einrückhebel). The handle and cranking claw are in a cranking housing (Andrehgehäuse) in the rear wall, closed by a hatch.
e) Kraftstofflagerung und -förderung, Bild 5  -  Fuel storage and delivery, Figure 5
The fuel is held in a 310-litre tank (Behälter) on the right of the engine. The filler opening (Einfüllöffnung) is reached through flaps in the rear armour, and the drain opening (Ablaßöffnung) through a cover in the hull floor. A bottom valve (Bodenventil) under the tank in the hull floor allows fuel to be drained from the vehicle in an emergency; it is worked from the fighting compartment by a hand lever on the partition wall.
A fuel-contents gauge (Kraftstoffvorratszeiger), consisting of an air pump and a pressure gauge (Druckmesser), shows the current fuel level. One stroke of the pump lowers the fuel level in a dip tube (Tauchrohr) that reaches to the bottom of the tank; the pressure head needed to displace the fuel column is read on the gauge and indicates the level. A safety valve (Sicherheitsventil) stops fuel from entering the instrument.
From the tank the fuel is delivered to the carburettor by the fuel pumps through a pipe containing a shut-off cock (Absperrhahn) that can be operated from the fighting compartment.
During operation, the two mechanical pumps deliver the fuel. They are fitted in the lower crankcase section (Kurbelgehäuse-Unterteil) of the engine …
(continued on page 15)

Original page 15
(continued from page 14 — mechanical fuel pumps)
… and are driven from the crankshaft via a cam disc (Nockenscheibe). They work as diaphragm pumps (Membranpumpen) with an inlet and an outlet valve. The suction stroke of the diaphragm is produced positively by the cam, the delivery stroke by a pressure spring (Druckfeder).
When the carburettor's float-needle valve (Schwimmernadelventil) is closed, the back-pressure in the fuel line prevents the pressure spring from pushing the diaphragm back, and the pump then idles. In this way the delivery of the fuel pumps regulates itself automatically according to the fuel demand at the carburettor.
For starting, an electric priming fuel pump (elektrische Anlaßkraftstoffpumpe) is provided; it serves only to deliver fuel from the tank to the carburettor when the lines are empty. It is electrically driven and likewise works as a diaphragm pump with inlet and outlet valves. Here the suction stroke is produced by an electromagnet (Elektromagnet) and the delivery stroke by a pressure spring. When the float-needle valve of the starting carburettor (Anlaßvergaser) is closed, this pump too delivers no fuel, owing to the back-pressure in the line.
The electric priming pump is switched on by a push-button on the instrument panel (Schaltbrett) at the start of a run. It must, however, only be operated for the short time before and during starting. The priming pump is connected to the electrical circuit only after the master switch key (Schaltschlüssel) has been inserted — i.e. it can only be set going by the push-button after that. Once the engine has fired, fuel delivery is taken over by the mechanical pumps.
5. Hauptkupplung und Gelenkwelle, Bild 2 und 6  -  Main clutch and cardan shaft, Figures 2 and 6
a) Hauptkupplung, Bild 6  -  Main clutch, Figure 6
A main clutch (Hauptkupplung) serves to interrupt the power flow (Kraftfluß) ahead of the gearbox.
This main clutch consists of a clutch housing (Kupplungsgehäuse) attached to the cylinder crankcase, containing the individual clutch parts. These are: an intermediate piece (Zwischenstück) bolted to the engine flywheel, together with the clutch cover (Kupplungsdeckel); a clutch shaft (Kupplungswelle); a release shaft (Ausrückwelle) mounted in the clutch housing, with release fork (Ausrückgabel) and release bearing (Ausrücklager); a clutch pressure spring (Kupplungsdruckfeder); three release levers (Ausrückhebel); a clutch pressure plate (Kupplungsdruckplatte); two clutch driving discs (Kupplungstreibscheiben); and three clutch discs (Kupplungsscheiben) slid onto the splined-shaft profile (Keilwellenprofil).

Original page 16
The main clutch is engaged by the clutch pressure spring (Kupplungsdruckfeder), which acts through the release bearing and the release levers on the clutch pressure plate, pressing together the clutch driving discs and the clutch discs (which are lined on both sides with clutch facings, Kupplungsbeläge). In this way the engine torque (Drehmoment) is transmitted — via the flywheel, the intermediate ring (Zwischenring) and the clutch cover, then via the two clutch driving discs (driven by dogs, Mitnehmer) and the three clutch discs — to the clutch shaft (Kupplungswelle).
The main clutch is disengaged by the release bearing which, when the clutch pedal (Kupplungsfußhebel) is depressed, is moved by the release shaft and release fork. This lifts the release levers off the clutch pressure plate, which then exerts no further pressure on the clutch discs and driving discs.
b) Gelenkwelle  -  Cardan shaft
The balanced cardan shaft (Gelenkwelle) transmits the engine torque from the main clutch to the gearbox. The journals of the universal joints (Kreuzgelenke) run in needle bearings (Nadellager).
On the clutch side the cardan shaft is bolted to the clutch shaft by a fixed intermediate flange (Zwischenflansch); on the gearbox side, to a flanged journal (Flanschzapfen). By means of an intermediate bush (Zwischenbuchse) this flanged journal engages in the first lower gearbox gear and can slide in the axial (longitudinal) direction.
6. Wechselgetriebe, Bild 7  -  Change-speed gearbox, Figure 7
The gearbox is a six-speed Aphon gearbox (Sechs-Gang-Aphon-Getriebe) with synchromesh (Gleichlaufeinrichtung). It has six forward gears and one reverse gear.
All gears except first and reverse have synchromesh.
The shafts are arranged one above the other; the reverse shaft (Rücklaufwelle) is on top. Power is fed in from the lower shaft and taken off through the main shaft (Hauptwelle), which lies on the same axis; at its free end the main shaft carries the bevel pinion (Kegelritzel).
The power path of the individual gears, the gear ratios (Übersetzungsverhältnisse) and the shift diagram (Schaltplan) can be seen from the table on Figure 9.
The gears are engaged by the gear lever (Schalthebel) fitted on the left-hand side of the gearbox.
A stop in the shift gate (Schaltführung) between fourth and fifth gear prevents unintentional shifting into fifth …
(continued on page 17)

Original page 17
(continued from page 16)
… gear when changing from second to third. Only after pressing the button on the gear knob (Schaltgriff) can fifth and sixth gear be engaged.
The reverse-gear position is secured by a spring-loaded lever operated by a hand lever.
Once a gear is engaged, all gears are interlocked against one another. The intermediate and end positions of the gear lever are secured by ball detents (Kugelsperren).
The gears for first to sixth are helically cut (schräg verzahnt) and permanently in mesh; those for reverse are straight-cut (geradeverzahnt) and are engaged only when shifting.
The synchromesh (Gleichlaufeinrichtung), whose arrangement and operation are described below, allows silent gear-changing.
Through dogs (Mitnehmer) the gear lever moves the selector rails (Schaltschienen), to which the shift linkage (Schaltgestänge) is connected. This linkage, which moves the synchromesh sliding sleeves (Gleichlaufschiebehülsen), is adjustable for precise setting; the setting is done during assembly.
The gearbox is lubricated by a gear pump (Zahnradpumpe) located inside the gearbox casing.
Oil is fed from the oil sump (Ölsumpf) via a gap filter (Spaltfilter) to the gears of the gearbox. The oil pump also supplies the steering gears (Lenkgetriebe) and the bevel drive (Kegeltrieb) with oil, which flows back from there into the gearbox. The gears for first and reverse are not connected to this oil circuit but dip into the oil sump.
The gap filter is set into the top of the casing and is removable. It is cleaned by turning the toggle (Knebel) provided on the right of the gearbox.
Gleichlaufeinrichtung — Anordnung  -  Synchromesh — Arrangement
The slidable selector sleeve 1 (Schaltmuffe) is connected to the gear lever through the shift linkage described above. Power is transmitted from the driven shaft 3 to the sliding sleeve 1 by the three keys 2 (Keile). Three sliding keys 4 (Schiebekeile) sit in grooves of the sliding sleeve 1 and are each releasably held in it by a spring-loaded catch 5 (Riegel); the three sliding keys also slide on flats of shaft 3. On both sides of the sliding sleeve 1 there are external dog teeth 6 (Mitnehmerzähne).

Original page 18
Gear 7 is supported on both sides in roller bearings. The bore of the gear has continuous dog teeth 8, which drive one set of synchronising discs 9 (Synchronisierungsscheiben) via external splines; the other set of synchromesh discs 10 (Gleichlaufscheiben) is driven by shaft 3 via internal splines. In each disc group, a disc 9 and a disc 10 alternate.
Wirkungsweise  -  Operation
The gear lever moves the selector sleeve in the direction of the arrow. In doing so, the spring-loaded catches 5 carry along the sliding keys 4 which, after covering the distance “S” (disc clearance, Scheibenspiel), press the synchromesh discs together until synchronism between shaft 3 and gear 7 is reached. As the sliding sleeve 1 moves on, the spring-loaded catches 5 are pushed out of the chamfered detents of the sliding keys 4. When the catches 5 have reached their uppermost position, a small gap still remains between the dog teeth 6 of the sliding sleeve 1 and the dog teeth 8 of gear 7. The synchronising process is thereby complete, and the dog teeth 6 are now slid into the teeth 8 of the gear, establishing the positive (force-locking) connection between shaft 3 and gear 7.
Gears that follow one another in the shift sequence lie opposite each other in pairs within the gearbox, so that on a gear change the sliding sleeve releases one gear and couples the other as it moves. The neutral position (Leerlaufstellung) is in the middle.
7. Kegeltrieb, Lenkgetriebe und Seitenwellen, Bild 8 bis 11  -  Bevel drive, steering gears and side shafts, Figures 8 to 11
Kegeltrieb, Lenkgetriebe mit Stützbremsen  -  Bevel drive, steering gears with support brakes
The bevel drive with the steering gears is flanged to the gearbox in a common housing. This housing has three chambers; the middle one contains the bevel drive (Kegeltrieb) with a reduction of 1.07 : 1. The other two chambers are arranged symmetrically about the middle chamber and contain the right and left steering gears (Lenkgetriebe).
Each steering gear consists of an epicyclic gear (Umlaufgetriebe) with a ratio of 1 : 1.391 and a following spur-gear reduction (Zahnradvorgelege) with a ratio of 1 : 1.862.
In straight-ahead driving, the epicyclic gears serve merely as reduction gears and, together with a spur-gear reduction, give an overall reduction of 1 : 2.6.

Original page 19
The two steering gears are completely identical in construction, so the description that follows is based on only one — namely the left-hand one as seen in the direction of travel.
a) Aufbau des Lenkgetriebes, Bild 8  -  a) Construction of the steering gear, Figure 8
On the bevel-gear shaft No. 1 (Kegelradwelle) running through the middle chamber sits the bell-shaped central ring gear No. 2 (Mittenhohlrad), rigidly connected to this shaft. Four planet gears No. 3 (Umlaufräder) mesh with it, carried in a planet carrier No. 4 (Umlaufradträger). The four planet gears mesh with a central sun gear No. 5 (Mittenvollrad), whose extended shaft passes through the bearing cover of the housing and carries the support-brake drum (Stützbremstrommel) on the outside. In this drum are the brake shoes (Bremsbacken) mounted on the bearing cover. A gear No. 6, firmly keyed to the planet carrier No. 4, meshes with the layshaft gear No. 7 (Vorgelegezahnrad). Through the side shaft (Seitenwelle) this layshaft gear is connected to the steering brake (Lenkbremse), and the final drive (Seitenvorgelege) to the drive sprocket (Triebrad).
b) Wirkungsweise  -  b) Operation
1. bei Geradeausfahrt, Bild 10  -  1. In straight-ahead driving, Figure 10
The support brake (Stützbremse) is applied and the steering brake released. The support-brake drum and the central sun gear No. 5 are thereby held stationary.
The drive turns the central ring gear No. 2 through the bevel drive. The planet gears No. 3 roll on the central sun gear No. 5 and turn the planet carrier No. 4, which drives the drive sprocket through the spur-gear reduction.
The support brake is set so that, after the tensioning lever (Nachspannhebel) on the brake spring is thrown over (Figures 23, 25), it transmits the maximum torque of the gearbox's first gear.
2. beim Lenken, Bild 11  -  2. When steering, Figure 11
The support brake is released and the steering brake applied. The smaller the curve to be driven, the more the steering brake must be braked. Here, for simplicity, a sharp turn is assumed — i.e. the left track is held completely stationary, with the steering brake firmly applied. The drive sprocket, the steering-brake drum (Lenkbremstrommel) and the planet carrier No. 4 are thereby held stationary.
The drive through the central ring gear No. 2 continues to turn; and since the planet carrier No. 4 is held stationary, the planet gears No. 3 turn only about their own axes and thereby cause the central sun gear No. 5, with the support-brake drum, to turn in the opposite direction.

Original page 20
By releasing the support brake — against which the power transmission is braced in straight-ahead running — the positive connection from the drive to the drive sprocket is broken.
c) Schmierung von Kegeltrieb und Lenkgetriebe, Bild 9  -  c) Lubrication of bevel drive and steering gears, Figure 9
The bevel drive and the spur-gear reductions of the two steering gears are lubricated with pressure oil (Drucköl); the epicyclic gear dips into the oil sump that forms.
The pressure oil is branched off from the pressure-lubrication line (Druckschmierleitung) of the gearbox and sprayed through nozzles (Düsen) into the gear meshes.
Through the bevel-drive housing (Kegeltriebgehäuse), which communicates with the oil space of the gearbox through wall openings, the oil flows back into the gearbox.
For pressure equalisation, a breather (Entlüfter) is provided on top of the bevel-drive housing.
d) Seitenwellen  -  d) Side shafts
From the steering gears the drive is passed on by two side shafts (Seitenwellen) to left and right, to the steering brakes and the final drives. The journals of the universal joints run in needle bearings.
8. Stütz- und Lenkbremse  -  8. Support brake and steering brake
a) Stützbremse, Bild 12 — Aufbau  -  a) Support brake, Figure 12 — Construction
The support brake is a servo internal-shoe brake (Servo-Innenbackenbremse) with two brake shoes.
The brake drum is firmly connected to the sun-gear shaft (Mittenvollradwelle) by a hub mounted in the bearing cover. The brake shoes, lined with woven-asbestos linings (Asbestgewebe), are held on the bearing cover.
An adjusting device (Nachstellvorrichtung) holds one pair of ends of the brake shoes together in an articulated manner, while the other ends bear against a retaining bolt (Haltebolzen) mounted in the bearing cover. Tension springs (Zugfedern) fitted on the bearing cover pull the shoes, when the brake is released, towards the centre of the drum and against the retaining bolt. Two further springs with retaining pins bring the shoes to bear axially against the bearing cover.
The centred position of the shoe system relative to the brake drum is set by a central centring device (Mittenzentrierung); the clearance between …
(continued on page 21)

Original page 21
(continued from page 20)
… shoes and drum is set by the adjusting device (see Section 25).
The brake shoes are actuated by the two cams of the rocker (Schwinge). Lugs of the brake lever (Bremshebel) — which pivots about the retaining bolt — engage in an elongated slot (Langloch) of this rocker.
The brake lever is under the action of the brake spring (Bremsfeder), which keeps the brake permanently applied. By a re-tensioning device (Nachspannvorrichtung) on the steering gear, the brake spring can be tensioned to a higher pull; this is done by throwing the spring-tensioning lever (Federspannhebel) downward. The brake is released by the steering lever (Lenkhebel).
Wirkungsweise — Lösen der Stützbremse  -  Operation — Releasing the support brake
When a steering lever is pulled, the force applied is transmitted to the brake lever. The brake lever and the rocker connected to it are set to “brake released” (Bremse lose); the brake shoes are thereby pulled away from the brake drum by the springs.
“The support-brake drum is free.”
Anziehen der Stützbremse  -  Applying the support brake
When the steering lever is returned to its initial position, the brake spring pulls the brake lever back up to “brake applied” (Bremse fest). The rocker is thereby turned and first presses the lower brake shoe, the “exciter shoe” (Erreger-Bremsbacke), against the drum. The exciter shoe, carried along in the direction of rotation by the friction force that arises, transmits this force through the adjusting device to the upper brake shoe, the “excited shoe” (Erregte Bremsbacke). This shoe too is thereby pressed against the drum — and more strongly than the exciter shoe.
During braking, the excited shoe braces itself against the retaining bolt, which thus takes up the braking circumferential force of both shoes. Depending on the clearance between shoe and drum, the exciter shoe moves more or less away from the retaining bolt; the rocker likewise travels along a little, owing to the elongated slot, and is thus relieved of the circumferential force.
“The support-brake drum is held fast.”

Original page 22
b) Lenkbremse, Bild 13 — Aufbau  -  b) Steering brake, Figure 13 — Construction
The steering brake is a generously sized servo internal-shoe brake with two brake shoes, mounted on a bearing housing No. 1 (Lagergehäuse) bolted to the side wall of the hull (Panzerwanne).
Through a flanged hub No. 2 (Flanschnabe) mounted in this housing, the brake drum No. 3 is connected to the pinion shaft No. 4 (Ritzelwelle) leading to the final drive. As long as the steering brake is not applied, the shoes are held against the bearing housing No. 1 by a ring of radially arranged tension springs.
In a retaining fork No. 5 (Haltegabel) — bolted to the bearing housing No. 1 via a flange so as to be adjustable — there is an adjusting device No. 6 (Nachstellvorrichtung), resiliently mounted on two compression springs, which, together with a tension spring, holds the upper ends of the brake shoes together in an articulated manner. The lower ends of the shoes are pulled against a stop on the housing by two tension springs hinged to the bearing housing.
The clearance between the brake shoes and the brake drum is set by means of the adjusting device (see Section 25).
The brake shoes are actuated by a conical brake wedge No. 8 (Bremskeil), connected to the inner and outer brake levers, which is drawn between the rollers fitted on the brake shoes and so spreads the shoes apart.
When the steering brake is applied — e.g. when driving tight curves — part of the engine power is destroyed and thereby converted into heat. To prevent an impermissible rise in temperature, the brake shoes are air-cooled.
For this purpose the brake shoes are made hollow and faced with individual lining blocks of special cast iron (Sondergußeisen), between which the cooling air is drawn into the shoes through slots. Through asbestos hoses (Asbestschläuche) the cooling air passes into the likewise hollow bearing housing, and via a suction line to the blower (Gebläse) mounted on the gearbox.
Wirkungsweise  -  Operation
When the steering lever is pulled, the force applied acts on the outer brake lever. The conical brake wedge No. 8 is drawn by the inner brake lever between the rollers of the brake shoes and forces them apart, pressing the shoes against the drum. Thereby the leading brake shoe in the direction of travel, the “exciter shoe No. 10” (Erreger-Bremsbacke), is — by the …
(continued on page 23)

Original page 23
(continued from page 22)
… friction force that arises — carried along somewhat in the direction of rotation of the brake drum, and transmits this drag force through the adjusting device No. 6 to the other brake shoe, the “excited shoe No. 11” (Erregte Bremsbacke). This shoe too is thereby pressed against the drum, but more strongly. During braking, the excited shoe No. 11 braces itself against the stop No. 7 (Anschlag) on housing No. 1, which thus takes up the braking circumferential force of both shoes.
Depending on the clearance between shoe and drum, shoe No. 10 moves more or less away from the stop on the housing; the articulated brake wedge adjusts itself accordingly.
9. Kraftübertragung zum Lenken  -  9. Power transmission for steering
Mech. Kraftübertragung, Bild 25  -  Mechanical power transmission, Figure 25
A steering linkage (Lenkgestänge) transmits the forces applied at the steering levers to the support and steering brakes. It consists essentially of the hand levers with transmission shafts (Übertragungswellen) and adjustable connecting rods (Verbindungsstangen).
A steering lever is provided on each side of the driver's seat, mounted in special bearing brackets (Lagerböcke).
The movement of each steering lever acts through an intermediate linkage on a corresponding roller lever (Rollenhebel) mounted on the steering gear. From there it is transmitted to the support brake's brake lever — designed as a cam lever (Kurvenhebel) — and, via connecting rods and two intermediate shafts (Zwischenwellen) mounted in the bow, to the outer brake levers of the steering brake.
Betätigen der Stützbremse  -  Operating the support brake
When a steering lever is pulled, the connecting linkage moves roller lever No. 2 so that the roller runs along the first cam track of roller lever No. 3 up to the crest B (Buckel). In doing so, cam lever No. 3 — held applied by the support-brake spring No. 4 (Stützbremsfeder) — is pushed down by about 14 mm, thereby releasing the support brake.
Betätigen der Lenkbremse  -  Operating the steering brake
On pulling steering lever No. 1 further, a pin provided in roller lever No. 2 takes the pull rod No. 16 (Zugstange) with it, and the steering brake is applied via the intermediate shaft mounted in the bow …
(continued on page 24 — Section 3)

Original page 24
(continued from page 23 — operating the steering brake)
… braked. As the roller runs along the second cam track of cam lever No. 3, the lever is held in the same position as when releasing the support brake — i.e. while the steering brake is being operated, the support brake is kept permanently released.
When the steering lever is returned to its initial position, the tension springs hinged to the brake levers also return the remaining linkage parts to their initial positions. The support brake is then applied again and the steering brake released.
To avoid lost motion (Totgang), a tension spring is provided on each steering lever.
10. Seitenvorgelege, Bild 14  -  10. Final drives, Figure 14
The two final drives (Seitenvorgelege) — spur-gear drives (Stirnradtriebe) with a reduction of 4 : 1 — are each mounted in a housing of armour material fitted on the outside of the hull. The large gear of the spur-gear drive is keyed to a flanged shaft (Flanschwelle) which passes through the housing and is sealed against ingress of sand or water by a grease-filled labyrinth seal (Labyrinthdichtung) and a sealing ring (Dichtring).
11. Laufwerk, Bild 15 bis 19  -  11. Running gear, Figures 15 to 19
On each side, the running gear comprises the drive sprocket (Triebrad), the six sprung road wheels (Laufrollen), the idler wheel (Leitrad), the three return rollers (Stützrollen) and the track (Gleiskette).
a) Triebrad, Leitrad mit Kettenspanner, Stützrollen  -  a) Drive sprocket, idler wheel with track tensioner, return rollers
The cast-steel drive sprocket (Figure 15) is bolted to the flanged shaft of the final drive and has two replaceable sprocket rings (Zahnkränze) of 21 teeth each, which engage in the track.
The idler wheel (Figure 16) is built as a spoked wheel with two welded-on rim rings (Felgenringe). The inner guide faces of these rim rings are hardened and serve to guide the track teeth laterally. The idler wheel turns in roller bearings about a crank axle (Kurbelachse) mounted at the rear of the hull. By swivelling this crank axle with a tensioning screw (Spannschraube) that engages a journal of the crank axle outside the hull, …
(continued on page 25)

Original page 25
(continued from page 24)
… the track tension is set. This tensioning screw is supported in a bearing socket (Lagerpfanne) at the rear. Between the head of the tensioning screw and the bearing socket there is a shear disc (Scherscheibe) which shears off if the track is overstrained.
The three return rollers (Figure 18) turn about fixed journals mounted in special brackets. They are built as twin wheels with removable solid-rubber tyres (Vollgummireifen), between which the track teeth are guided laterally by guide rings (Führungsringe).
b) Laufrollen und Stabfedern, Bild 17 und 18  -  b) Road wheels and torsion bars, Figures 17 and 18
The road wheels are likewise built as twin wheels and consist of two sheet-steel discs welded to a hub, onto which rims with solid-rubber tyres are fitted. Here too the track teeth are guided laterally by inner guide rings. Each road wheel turns on a journal pressed into a swing arm (Schwingarm) mounted in the hull. A torsion bar (Drehstabfeder) — a bar of spring steel with two serrated heads — engages by one head in the swing arm, while the other head is held in a socket (Nuß) beside the bearing of the opposite swing arm on the other side of the hull. The upward travel of the swing arms is limited by rubber bump stops (Gummianschläge). To take up the laterally arising forces, each swing arm is guided in a slide track (Gleitbahn) fixed to the hull.
c) Stoßdämpfer, Bild 19  -  c) Shock absorbers, Figure 19
To damp pitching oscillations (Nickschwingungen), the front and rear swing arm on each side of the vehicle is fitted with a shock absorber.
The shock absorber — Hemscheidt type — is a single-acting hydraulic (oil-pressure) damper that damps the downward movements of the road wheel. It consists essentially of a cylinder in which a piston rod with piston moves to and fro. Between the piston and the cylinder cover there is oil, which must be displaced when the cylinder moves down and thus opposes the piston movement. This resistance is set by a high-pressure valve (Hochdruckventil) in the piston, through which the oil must flow as the damper is extended. When the damper is compressed, the oil flows back at low counter-pressure through a low-pressure valve (Niederdruckventil) into the space between the piston and cylinder cover.

Original page 26
Since, on compression of the damper, the volume displaced by the piston is greater than that released on the piston-rod side, a jacket-shaped reservoir (mantelförmiger Vorratsbehälter) is provided to take up the surplus oil — and, on extension of the damper, to serve as a reservoir for the now-missing oil.
This reservoir communicates with the cylinder through a suction valve and a delivery valve in the cylinder base.
d) Gleiskette, Bild 1  -  d) Track, Figure 1
The tracks consist of individual interlocking track links (Kettenglieder) joined to one another by track pins (Kettenbolzen). The track pitch (Kettenteilung) is 120 mm. The track pins are held on the inner side by a head and on the outer side by a track-pin retainer (Kettenbolzensicherung).
The track is unlubricated.
To achieve the greatest possible grip, the track links have grouser bars (Greiferleisten) and are made open-worked (durchbrochen) for self-cleaning. The teeth of the drive sprockets engage in the side gaps of the track links. In the centre of the track links a guide tooth is cast on for the lateral guidance of the track.

The linkage consists of the hand- and foot-lever parts with their associated shafts and adjustable connecting rods.

The starting device is operated by a hand lever mounted to the right of the driver.

The chassis is equipped with a 12 V Bosch lighting and starting system.

C. Anweisung für das Schmieren  -  C. Lubrication instructions
18. Schmieren im Betrieb, Bild 21 und 22  -  18. Lubrication in service, Figures 21 and 22
Correct lubrication ensures good running and a long service life of the vehicle.
The oil and grease grades, the lubrication points, lubrication intervals and lubricants are given in the lubrication and maintenance instructions (Schmier- und Pflegeanweisung), Figure 22, and the lubrication and maintenance chart (Schmier- und Pflegeplan), Figure 21.
The running distances given here, after which re-lubrication is due, are only guide values.
Depending on the condition or operating conditions of the vehicle (defective seals, muddy ground, loose sand, heavy rain), oil must be topped up, or the lubrication points greased, correspondingly earlier.
a) Ölschmierung — Motor  -  a) Oil lubrication — Engine
Using the dipstick (Ölmeßstab), which shows a maximum and minimum permissible level, check the oil level in the oil tank daily and top up as required.
Avoid too much oil, otherwise malfunctions occur.
At an oil change, drain the old oil with the engine warm — after removing the floor flaps in the hull — from the drain openings of the oil tank and the crankcase; briefly start the engine so that the old oil also runs out of the lines.
Fill in fresh oil and briefly run the engine again so that the empty lines are refilled. Then bring the oil level back to maximum by topping up again.
At the oil change, the oil filter must also be cleaned at the same time.
Wechsel-, Kegel- und Lenkgetriebe  -  Gearbox, bevel drive and steering gears
Drain the oil through the drain plug on the gearbox and the two plugs under the steering gears.

The plug for the gearbox is accessible after removing the cover in the hull floor.
Fill oil through the side filler opening. The oil level is limited by the overflow of the side filler opening.
At the oil change, clean the oil filter and the breathers (Entlüfter) on the gearbox and steering gears at the same time.
Lenkbremse, Bild 13  -  Steering brake, Figure 13
The bearing housing of the steering-brake drum has no special oil drain; the oil must therefore be sucked out with a syringe.
Fill fresh oil into the filler opening on the bearing neck up to the edge of the opening.
Clean the breather at the same time. Change the oil each time the brake lining is replaced.
ATTENTION! No oil must reach the brake linings or the inner surface of the brake drum!
Seitenvorgelege  -  Final drives
Drain the old oil from the lower stub and fill fresh oil into the housing through the front filler opening up to its edge.
Hauptantriebsscheibe für die Lüfter  -  Main drive pulley for the fans
Fill half full with oil. Turn the drive pulley so that the plugs face each other horizontally.

Grease the cardan shafts between the main clutch and gearbox, and the side shafts between the steering gear and steering brake, with grease gun III DB 70 (Schmierrohr III DB 70).
When the rear armour is occasionally removed, also grease the cardan shaft to the fan drive.
b) Fettschmierung  -  b) Grease lubrication
At lubrication points greased with a grease gun (Schmierpresse), keep pressing in fresh grease until the used grease …

20. Panzerwanne  -  20. Armoured hull
Keep entry and exit hatches, hand-hole covers, bottom valves etc. free of sand and dirt, and always bolt them up with the associated, undamaged seals so that they stay watertight when fording. Oil the joints from time to time and rub the rubber seals with talc.
21. Motor  -  21. Engine
Start the cold engine with the inertia starter (Schwungkraftanlasser). A hand crank inserted in the cranking claw is to be turned anticlockwise by two men, slowly at first, then increasingly fast.
Only after reaching about 60 rpm (i.e. one turn per second) is the handle beside the cranking claw to be pulled out to the stop and held until the engine fires.
During starting, the driver must operate the lever of the starting device (Anlaßvorrichtung); once the engine fires, release it immediately and give throttle.
The electric starter is to be used only with the engine warm, never with a cold engine.
ATTENTION! The electric and the inertia starter must never be used while the engine is still turning, nor both together, otherwise the starter pinions are damaged.
Anlassen des Motors  -  Starting the engine
1.) Move the gear lever to the neutral (middle) position.
2.) Insert the switch key into the switch box; the red lamp must then light up.
3.) Operate the push-button of the electric fuel pumps (about 20 seconds).
(continued on page 36)

Original page 36
4.) With a cold engine, press the lever of the starting device down until the engine fires, without operating the accelerator pedal.
5.) Depress the clutch pedal.
6.) Crank the inertia starter and engage it by pulling out the handle.
7.) After the engine fires, release the clutch pedal slowly.
8.) Let the engine warm up for a while at about 1000 to 1200 rpm. It is advisable to close the air-regulating flaps temporarily.
At high outside temperature, or with thin gear oil, operating the clutch pedal during starting is not necessary.
When difficult terrain calls for slow driving and the outside temperature is high, it is advantageous — for good cooling — to run the engine at high revs and engage a correspondingly low gear.
The engine may run at a normal speed of 2600 rpm. Over-revving must be avoided. The red-marked range of the tachometer — 2600 to 3200 rpm — should, if possible, not be used. This is especially important when the engine is also used as a brake going downhill, where the speed should be at most 2200 to 2400 rpm.
After heavy use in the field, the exhaust valves and spark plugs may glow. If the engine is then stopped at once, it may run on because the fuel-air mixture ignites on the glowing parts.
In this case it is advisable to let the engine run for about a minute; the cooling water then keeps circulating and the hot parts can cool. If the engine has still not stopped after this time with the ignition switched off, faster cooling can likewise be achieved — with the ignition off — by giving full throttle.
Einfahren  -  Running-in
New engines, and engines overhauled at one of the Maybach repair workshops, need not be run in. It is only necessary to ensure that the coolant temperature is at least 50 °C at the start.

Original page 37
Overhauled engines that could not be run in are to be run in for five hours at idle with the vehicle stationary, and then over 200 km at max. 2000 rpm.
For major repairs the engine is to be removed and sent to the firm Maybach-Motorenbau, Friedrichshafen a. B., or to their nearest repair workshop.
Ausbau (Sonderwerkzeug III DB 20)  -  Removal (special tool III DB 20)
Before removal, drain the cooling water through the drain plug, accessible after removing the bottom cover at the rear of the hull.
The following are to be disconnected:
Lines to: radiator, fuel tank, air filter, exhaust, air line to the dynamo, and electrical leads;
Linkages to: carburettor, starting device, oil filter, inertia starter, and cardan shaft to the fan drive.
In the fighting compartment: tunnel top part (Tunneloberteil), linkage to the carburettor and starting carburettor, tunnel head (Tunnelkopf) and cardan shaft. This is, however, only sufficient if a lifting device (Aufhängevorrichtung) is available for removing the engine that allows it to be lifted — raised on the water-pump side — obliquely upward out of the vehicle.
If no such removal device is available, the clutch must additionally be released from the engine.
Then the left radiator is to be removed first, after which the engine can be lifted upward once the fastening bolts are undone.
Ölfilter  -  Oil filter
Before the engine is first started, clean the oil filter by depressing the clutch pedal 5 to 8 times. At each oil change, carefully remove the filter bowl (Filterglocke) and clear away the accumulated dirt. At the same time check the condition of the filter discs (Filterlamellen): they must all be evenly spaced and must not be pressed together in one place and spread apart in another. If larger gaps appear, larger dirt particles may have jammed between them; these are then to be removed carefully, …
(continued on page 38)

Reinigen von außen  -  Cleaning from outside
In case of dust fouling, blow off from both sides with compressed air. In case of oil and dust fouling, remove the crust by brushing or dabbing with Henkel P3 solution and then blow off with compressed air.
Reinigen von innen  -  Cleaning from inside
About every 4 weeks, flush the radiators with a Henkel P3 solution. To do this, drain the cooling water, dissolve 5 to 6 tablespoons of P3 in a full bucket of water, pour it into the radiators and keep topping up with fresh water until the radiators are full. After a few days, drain the P3 solution and refill the radiators with clean water.
If the cooling water boils very readily, in the morning pour ¼ kg of P3 — previously dissolved — into the empty radiators. After the vehicle has been driven during the day, drain the hot water, flush the radiators well and refill with fresh water.

The drive belts of the fans should run with a slight pre-tension and are to be re-tensioned regularly in service. This tensioning is done by adjusting the main drive pulley at the rear of the vehicle. After adjustment, retighten and lock the nuts of the adjusting screw.
The disc clutches (Scheibenkupplungen) of the fans are set at the factory. The compression springs must not be re-tensioned.
Keep the drive belts and disc clutches free of oil and grease.

Removal of the radiators: To remove the right radiator, the fan must first be taken out.
Installation of the fans: When installing the fans, ensure that the strong, rounded edge of the fan blade faces the guide apparatus (Leitapparat).

25. Einstellen und Behandeln der Stütz- und Lenkbremse  -  25. Adjusting and servicing the support and steering brakes

Stützbremse, Bild 23 und 25  -  Support brake, Figures 23 and 25
The sign that re-adjustment is necessary is the distance “C” between the roller of roller lever No. 2 and cam lever No. 3. If this distance has decreased to about 2 mm, the brake lining has worn by 0.5 mm and brake spring No. 4 no longer has the tension needed to brake the support brake. The support brake then slips on steeper gradients and must be re-adjusted.
A temporary improvement can be achieved by keeping tensioning lever No. 5 — which must always be thrown back when driving in 1st gear — thrown over also in the higher gears. This is, however, only a short-term expedient, because the support brake soon slips again and the force at steering lever No. 1 rises above 30 kg.
Before adjusting the brakes, check the positions of the steering levers. In the initial position, the left steering lever must be far enough forward that a gap of about 50 mm can be measured between the shaft and the guard plate of the side cardan shaft. The right steering lever must be set parallel to the left. Otherwise, before adjusting the support brakes, adjust the hand-lever stops accordingly.
Einstellen der Stützbremse — a) Nachstellen im Betrieb  -  Adjusting the support brake — a) Re-adjustment in service
1.) Pull steering lever No. 1 until the roller of roller lever No. 2 is behind crest B.
2.) Undo the retaining screws of adjusting device No. 6 with the long socket spanner III DB 144.

Original page 52
3.) Bring the brake shoes to a light contact with the brake drum by turning the square of adjusting device No. 6 clockwise with socket spanner III DB 51 — i.e. the shoes may just touch the drum. (The drum can still be moved by hand, with some effort, to move the backlash present in the steering gear to and fro. In winter, with thick gear oil, turning the drum takes greater force.)
4.) Retighten the retaining screws of adjusting device No. 6 firmly and turn its square back anticlockwise by two turns — 8 detent positions = 0.3 mm shoe clearance.
b) Neueinstellen nach Ausbau  -  b) Fresh setting after removal
1.) Hang in the spring and, with the steering lever pulled and the ball-collar nut No. 7 released, set it to the stamped length dimension, Figure 25.
2.) Set the brake shoes as under a), points 2 to 4.
3.) Raise angle lever No. 8 by hand to the stop and turn ball-collar nut No. 7 in until the play between it and the angle lever has dropped to about one tenth of a millimetre. Secure the ball-collar nut.
4.) When the steering lever is pushed forward again, cam lever No. 3 is now pulled about 14 mm by brake spring No. 4, whereby the spring tension drops to about 55 kg.
Prüfung 1  -  Check 1
With a properly adjusted support brake, the roller of roller lever No. 2 must not touch cam lever No. 3. Contact may occur only after the free travel of steering lever No. 1, i.e. about 24 mm before cam crest “B”. With the steering lever pushed forward, there is a gap of about 5 mm between the roller of roller lever No. 2 and the support-brake drum.
The force at steering lever No. 1 should not exceed 9 to 12 kg, and about 20 kg with tensioning lever No. 5 thrown over. Otherwise check the linkage for free movement.
Einstellen der Lenkbremse, Bild 24 und 25  -  Adjusting the steering brake, Figures 24 and 25
1.) Undo the retaining screws of adjusting device No. 9 with the long hexagon-socket spanner III DB 132.

Original page 53
2.) Bring the brake shoes to contact with the brake drum by turning the square of adjusting device No. 9 clockwise with socket spanner III DB 109 — i.e. the shoes may just touch the drum.
3.) Retighten the retaining screws of the adjusting device firmly. The brake shoes are thereby freshly centred.
4.) Turn adjusting device No. 9 back — anticlockwise — by twelve turns of the spanner. The brake shoes are thereby set to 0.5 mm clearance from the drum.
5.) With the steering lever at rest, set adjusting device No. 10 on the outer brake lever so that brake wedge No. 11 lies lightly against the rollers, but without play.
Prüfung 2  -  Check 2
If steering lever No. 1 is pulled so that the roller of roller lever No. 2 stands at point B — but at most 2 to 3 mm behind point B — then bolt No. 17 must begin to move in the elongated slot, taking pull rod No. 16 to the steering brake with it, Figure 35.
Behandeln der Bremsen  -  Servicing the brakes
The brake linings must be free of oil and grease, otherwise the braking effect is considerably reduced.
When greasing, therefore, take strict care that no oil or grease reaches the linings or the inner surface of the drum.
If the linings become oiled, the shoes must be replaced if thorough washing with fuel does not help. Remove the cause of the oil leak.
Einbau neuer Bremsbacken  -  Fitting new brake shoes
To achieve full seating of the brake linings on the drum and even clearance when the brakes are released, the linings are machined exactly to match the brake drum. When the linings are worn, the brake shoes — and if necessary also the brake drum — are to be sent to the responsible Army ordnance depot (Heereszeugamt), which returns the shoes with new linings, ready riveted and machined.
Re-lining the brake shoes by the troops requires extensive experience and equipment and is therefore not permitted.

Original page 54
On the steering brake, the wear of the special-cast-iron lining may be driven until the lining segments break; but note that flaking linings can render the brake ineffective and cause it to block. For this reason the drums are to be removed from time to time and the thickness of the brake linings checked — especially at the excited shoes: on the support brake the upper brake shoe No. 1, on the steering brake the brake shoe No. 13 lying at the rear in the direction of travel. At the same time, clean and grease the brake.
The lining of the support-brake shoes consists of an asbestos fabric and must be renewed when it is so worn that the rivet heads rub on the drum.
On the steering brake, fit the brake shoes so that brake shoe No. 12, with the short brake lining, is at the front (as seen in the direction of travel), and brake shoe No. 13, with the long brake lining, at the rear.

Check the linkage from time to time for free (non-binding) movement, especially when — with the brakes properly adjusted — the forces to be applied at the steering levers rise unduly.
When releasing the support brake (roller in front of crest “B” of cam lever No. 3), the hand-lever force must not exceed about 12 kg. At the start of braking in the steering brake (roller just behind crest “B” of cam lever No. 3), the hand-lever force must not be higher than about 16 kg.
When throwing the spring-tensioning lever down (rearward), the hand-lever forces must not rise above about 20 kg when releasing the support brake, nor above 22 kg at the start of braking of the steering brake.

Remove the closing cap of the idler wheel, undo the nut, take off the D-washer, shim washer and angle ring of the roller bearing, and carefully pull the idler wheel off. Undo the clamping ring on the upper journal of the crank axle, the tension band (Spannband), the screws of the crank-axle flange and the nut on the shaft end in the vehicle; then pull the crank axle out of the bearing sleeve.
Installation: Draw the sealing rings over the bearing sleeve, insert the crank axle carefully and assemble the crank-axle flange. Draw the roller-bearing outer rings, spacer sleeve and sealing ring into the idler wheel. Fill the hub between the roller-bearing rings with grease, grease the sealing ring well and carefully push the idler wheel onto the crank-axle journal. Fit the angle ring, shim rings and D-washer, tighten the nut well and secure with a locking plate. Fill the closing cap with grease and screw it on.
Aus- und Einbau der Scherscheibe  -  Removal and installation of the shear disc
If the shear disc (Scherscheibe) has sheared, the track need not be removed to fit a new one. Clamp a wooden block between the last road wheel and the idler wheel and, after unscrewing the screw sleeve, turn the tensioning screw out to the rear. Replace the shear disc and refit the tensioning screw. It is advisable to put a few drops of oil on the bearing face of the shear disc.
c) Stützrollen, Laufrollen, Schwingarm, Drehstabfedern und Stoßdämpfer  -  c) Return rollers, road wheels, swing arm, torsion bars and shock absorbers
Special tools III DB 92, 100 to 103, 112, 113, 121, 128, 130
When installing the return rollers, road wheels, swing arms, torsion bars and shim plates, ensure they are refitted exactly the same way and in the same place as before removal. Thus, for example, the torsion bars of the swing arms, or the roller-bearing outer and inner rings of the road wheels, must on no account be interchanged.

Original page 58
It is therefore advisable to mark all parts before removal so that faultless installation in the same place is ensured.
For removing and installing individual road wheels, torsion bars and swing arms, as well as the shock absorbers, the track need not be taken off. If, however, several road wheels, torsion bars or swing arms are removed, it is advisable to place the vehicle on stands (Böcke) and take off the track.
Stützrollen  -  Return rollers
Removal: Take off the return roller together with its bearing bracket (Lagerbock) and dismantle.
Installation: First assemble the return roller with its axle, then draw the axle into the bearing bracket. If necessary, correct the position of the return roller relative to the track with shim plates (Paßbleche) under the base.
Laufrollen  -  Road wheels
Removal: Raise the swing arm with a jack until the track teeth no longer hinder the subsequent pulling-off of the road wheel. When removing the 1st and 6th road wheel, the track must be released, the idler wheel set as far forward as possible, and the track pressed down with a crowbar while the road wheel is pulled off. Unscrew the closing cap of the road wheel, undo the castellated nut, take off the D-washer, shim rings and angle ring, and carefully pull off the road wheel.
Installation: Draw the roller-bearing outer rings, clamping ring and sealing ring into the road wheel. Fill the hub between the roller-bearing rings with grease, grease the sealing ring well and carefully push the road wheel on. Fit the shim rings, angle ring and D-washer. Tighten the nut and split-pin it. Fill the cover with grease and screw it on. Screw on the closing cap and secure the screw with locking plates.

Stoßdämpfer, Bild 19  -  Shock absorbers, Figure 19
Special tools in the workshop vehicle: fitting device for shock-absorber bolts III DB 137; extracting device for shock-absorber bolts III DB 138.
A well-functioning shock absorber must, on a run over poor terrain or a longer stretch of potholes, reach about 50 °C (felt by hand); on a run over normal grass sward it becomes hand-warm. If a shock absorber stays cold, either a lack of oil or a defect is to be inferred.
When fitting a new shock absorber, ensure that the groove in the upper joint head faces the vehicle.
Nachfüllen der Stoßdämpfer  -  Topping up the shock absorbers
The oil level must be checked every 500 km.
After removing the closing cover on the outer protective tube, the filler opening — closed by a screw — becomes visible with the vehicle under normal load. Through this opening the oil level can be checked and oil topped up as needed. Under normal load the oil must reach the lower edge of the filler opening. If, with an unfavourable position of the running gear, the oil filler screw is not accessible, the vehicle must be brought into the normal position.
Schwingarm und Stabfedern — Ausbau, Bild 30 (Sonderwerkzeug III DB 92, 100 bis 103, 121, 129)  -  Swing arm and torsion bars — Removal, Figure 30 (special tools III DB 92, 100 to 103, 121, 129)
Fig. 30/1 — Take off the road wheel. From the bearing socket (Lagernuß) of the torsion bar of the relevant swing arm, after removing the floor cover, pull out the two retaining pins (III DB 100).
Fig. 30/2 — Unscrew the closing cap from the swing arm (III DB 129) and unscrew the nuts and washers from the torsion-bar journal. Pull out the torsion bar (III DB 101) and press the bearing socket upward out.
Fig. 30/3 — Pull the swing arm out of the bearing (III DB 92, 102).
Fig. 30/4 — After removing the rear slotted nut on the swing-arm bearing leg, press off the swing-arm flange, needle bearing, shim washers and spacer sleeves (III DB 103).

Einbau, Bild 31  -  Installation, Figure 31
Fig. 31/4 and 1 — Fit the bearing socket (III DB 112). Draw the swing-arm flange, needle bearing, shim washers and spacer sleeves onto the swing arm.
Fig. 31/2 — Check the swing-arm bearing bush (of Novotext); if necessary remove it, press in a new bush and ream with reamer (III DB 113).
Fig. 31/3 — Fit the swing arm in the bearing and, so that the sealing ring is not damaged, use tool (III DB 103). Bring the swing arm to the correct inclination relative to the hull floor with the setting gauge (Einstellehre) (III DB 121) and insert the torsion bar.
This setting gauge is in the special-tool set of the repair workshop.
Should the setting gauge, however, not be at hand, the correct inclination of the swing arms relative to the hull floor can be set as follows:
The swing arm inserted in the bearing is raised until the centre of the road-wheel journal is 145 mm, measured downward, from the hull floor. In this position the torsion bar is then to be fitted.
To keep to this dimension as exactly as possible, it is advisable to lay a straight strip under the hull floor so that it projects a little at the side. The required dimension of 145 mm (upper edge of measuring strip to centre of road-wheel journal) can then be observed exactly.
Press the retaining pins into the bearing socket so far that the thread end — allowing for later pulling-out when removing the torsion bars — still projects about 6 mm; bolt on the floor cover.
When fitting new torsion bars, only as many shim washers may be pushed onto the torsion-bar journal as leaves a play of 0.2 to 0.5 mm between the thrust washer and its bearing face in the swing arm. Fit the nuts on the torsion-bar journal and screw the closing cap into the swing arm (III DB 121).
After assembly, check the return rollers and road wheels for exact alignment (III DB 130). Tool (III DB 130/1) is clamped between the upper teeth of the drive sprocket, tool (III DB 130/2) between the flange rings of the …

(continued from page 60)
… idler wheel, and tensioned by the notches of a thin cord. With tool (III DB 130/3), which is clamped one after another between the flange rings of the return roller, the exact alignment can then be checked.
When checking the road wheels, proceed in the same way; only for this purpose the drive sprocket and idler wheel must be turned downward with the corresponding tools.
d) Gleiskette  -  d) Track
Special tool III DB 54, in the chassis on the fuel tank.
Lay the tracks so that, on the section lying on the ground, the links with the three eyes point forward in the direction of travel.
The track-pin retainers (Kettenbolzensicherungen) must be on the outside and are to be bent so that, on the track section lying on the ground, the lower bend of the retainer points forward. With a slipping track the retainers cannot then be broken off.
Auflegen der Gleiskette  -  Laying the track
Set the idler wheel as far forward as possible. Lay out both tracks at track gauge in front of the vehicle; the heads of the track pins face the hull. Drive the vehicle onto the track until the front edge of the drive sprocket roughly meets the front end of the track. Pull the rear ends of the track over the idler wheel and the return rollers, and bring both ends together over the drive sprocket with the track-tensioning device III DB 54. Drive in the pins and secure.
Tension the track with the tensioner so that it sags about 40 mm between the return rollers. Over-tight tracks cause increased rolling resistance; too-loose ones tend to derail. If the adjustment travel of the idler wheel is not enough to tension the track, a new track link must be fitted or one removed.
Auswechseln eines Kettenbolzens  -  Replacing a track pin
Drive the vehicle so that the link in which the track pin is to be replaced is on the upper part of the drive sprocket. Release the track. Chisel off the track-pin retainer and knock the damaged track pin out from outside to inside with a track pin. With a new track pin, head towards …
(continued on page 62)

Original page 62
(continued from page 61)
… the hull, knock out the track pin used for driving out, and secure the new track pin with a track-pin retainer. Tension the track.
When removing a badly damaged track pin, it is advisable to use tool III DB 54 and to turn the damaged pin about 90° before knocking it out.
Abnutzung der Kette  -  Track wear
The wear of the track pins and eyes on the track links caused by driving results in an increase of the track pitch. The wear may be allowed until, when driving forward, the forward flanks of the drive sprocket hinder the track from running on. In this case the track pins are to be renewed and, so that the still-unworn tooth flanks come into engagement with the track, the sprocket rings of each drive sprocket are to be interchanged.
When fitting several new track links, ensure that they are not fitted directly one behind another but distributed evenly over the whole track.
If a vehicle continually runs off to one side, a one-sided stretching of the track is present (e.g. overloading of one track). A remedy is to divide both tracks into 12 to 15 parts and interchange corresponding parts (about every second section) of the two tracks. At the same time, worn track pins, track links and sprocket rings are to be replaced.
Verhalten bei Kettenentgleisung  -  Action in the event of track derailment
If a track runs off the idler wheel to the outside, drive forward and apply the steering brake of the non-derailed side.
If a track runs off the idler wheel to the inside, drive forward and apply the steering brake of the derailed side.
If the lower part of the track derails, first remove a track pin at the idler wheel. By appropriate forward and reverse driving, the derailed lower track run is brought back into the normal position. Then set the idler wheel forward, fit the track pin and finally bring the track back to normal tension.

E. Sondervorschriften  -  E. Special instructions
29. Abdichtringe  -  29. Oil seals (sealing rings)
The oil seals (Abdichtringe) must be laid in oil at room temperature for a few minutes immediately before installation.
The shaft edge over which the seal is pushed must be chamfered and deburred. If the edge cannot be chamfered, the seal must be slid onto the shaft by means of an installation sleeve (Einbauhuelse).
The chamfered edge of the sealing lip (Manschette) must not be folded back during assembly.
The seal is pressed into the receiving housing with the help of a drawing-in mandrel (Einziehdorn).
If the seal is intended to prevent oil and grease escaping from a drive unit, it must be fitted with the lettered face of its housing directed inwards. If it is intended to prevent the entry of dust and dirt, the lettered face must be directed outwards.
30. Fernthermometer  -  30. Remote (distance) thermometer
During installation and removal, all tugging and yanking must be avoided. This applies especially to the points where the thin line passes into the sensing bulb (Tauchkoerper) or the indicator housing, since experience shows that breakage most readily occurs here through careless handling.
The coiled line must under no circumstances be pulled apart, but must be unwound carefully. The indicator instrument, the remote line (Fernleitung) and the heating shaft form a single, inseparable whole. The remote line is a liquid-filled tube which must neither be cut nor crushed, since otherwise the connection between the sensing bulb and the indicator housing is lost.

Original page 64
30. Fernthermometer (Fortsetzung)  -  30. Remote thermometer (continued)
The copper tube on which the union nut is movably arranged should, as far as possible, not be bent. During installation, sharp bends and kinks must therefore be avoided under all circumstances. Where the routing changes direction, the tightest bend permitted is a radius of 3 cm, unless one wishes to risk fracturing the line. Excess length of the remote line must be laid in large loops. The sensing bulb and remote line must never be handled with tools (pliers and the like), since pinching can cause hairline cracks through which the liquid escapes and the instrument ceases to work.
It is advisable to make short curves at both ends of the remote line, so that tearing of the line is prevented when expansion occurs between the measuring points and the instrument panel.
The remaining line must be laid firmly with pipe clamps at intervals of 25 to 30 cm, since natural vibration of this line leads to breakage in the long run.
The indicator instruments must never be opened by unqualified hands, and repairs may only be carried out by the manufacturer. Broken glass faces must be replaced immediately.
After removal of the thermometer, the measuring point must be closed off with the hexagon screw plug (Sechskantverschlussschraube) supplied with it.

F. Fahrvorschrift  -  F. Driving instructions
31. Allgemeines  -  31. General
Before every move-off, ensure that the foot brake is released, otherwise fractures of the side (final-drive) shafts (Seitenwellen) will occur.
Do not move off below a coolant temperature of 50 degrees.
The engine must not run above its normal speed of 2600 rpm, i.e. do not drive in the range of the red field on the tachometer. Change gear in good time.
When changing gear the engine must likewise not be over-revved, otherwise valve-spring breakages result.
The gears must be shifted briskly. When shifting, observe the action of the synchromesh (Gleichlaufvorrichtung): take the gear out quickly; during the shift pause do not leave the gear lever in the neutral (middle) position, but bring the new gear up against the synchromesh with light pressure and then engage it. Change up without double-declutching. When changing down, blip the throttle (Zwischengas) and double-declutch.
Do not let the clutch slip any longer than absolutely necessary. When driving, do not rest the foot on the clutch pedal, so that the release ring (Ausrueckring) does not rub unnecessarily.
First gear is to be used only for steep slopes up and down, ditches, gullies, craters and blind terrain. In first gear, and also when the support brake (Stuetzbremse) slips, the re-tensioning lever on the support-brake spring must be moved towards the driver (down). On level ground, move off in 2nd or 3rd gear. Drive at high speed only on open, clear stretches. At all blind spots, and especially when overtaking, change down in good time and drive carefully.
When difficult terrain calls for slow travel, the engine should be run at a higher speed for the sake of good cooling, and the correspondingly lower gear engaged.

Original page 66
31. Allgemeines (Fortsetzung) - Lenken  -  31. General (continued) - Steering
For changes of direction there are two steering methods:
a) Slight deviations from the direction of travel and long, gentle curves are driven by releasing the support brake - lightly pulling the steering lever on the side of the desired direction - while giving throttle at the same time. Pull the steering lever only far enough that the support brake is fully released but the steering brake (Lenkbremse) is not yet applied.
b) Curves that must be driven by applying the steering brake are to be negotiated not as a circular arc but as a polygon; that is, the steering brake is applied and released in continuous alternation with the steering lever. This ensures better cooling of the steering brake, and since every braking action consumes power, the engine has enough time to recover.
The better the driver, the cooler the steering brakes.
When steering, always give throttle as a matter of principle. A power reserve must be available when steering. Therefore change down in good time, but do not let the engine speed drop below 2000 rpm.
Turns on the spot (pivot turns) are to be avoided; they may only be carried out in an emergency.
On good roads, which usually offer little grip to full-tracked vehicles, there is a danger of over-steering the vehicle. Increased care is required on such roads out of consideration for other road users.
A steering reversal (Lenkwechsel) - left steering lever = right turn - occurs when, while coasting down from high speed with the engine braking, one steers only with the support brake released. This steering reversal is to be avoided on the road as far as possible. It is expedient to change down in good time, steer normally and, if necessary, give more throttle.
During practice runs, care must be taken to choose the route so that turns can be made to both sides, so that one steering brake is not overheated and the necessary cooling times are available.
In great heat (about 30 degrees in the shade) do not drive continuously at full throttle, so that boiling of the radiator is avoided. In good time ...

Original page 67
... change into a low gear and drive at low speed.
Fahren im Gelaende  -  Driving off-road (in terrain)
To spare the vehicle and to save fuel, the ground must be selected with the utmost care with regard to steering movements. Steer where possible at points of least resistance (small undulations, firm ground). On damp ploughed soil, swamp or deep sand, steer sparingly. Do not over-steer curves. Always drive with foresight.
Good driving technique reduces the running costs and the wear (damage) of this equipment to a noticeable degree.
Befahren von Steilhaengen  -  Negotiating steep slopes
a) Driving uphill (Aufwaertsfahren)
1) Set the vehicle to the slope square-on (at a right angle).
2) Steer as little as possible.
3) Do not over-rev the engine.
4) If the tracks slip, give only enough throttle that the engine just pulls through (about 1600 to 1800 rpm).
b) Driving downhill (Abwaertsfahren)
1) Approach at a right angle to the top edge of the slope.
2) Engage the gear in which you would have driven up the same slope.
3) At the moment of tipping over the edge, take the foot off the clutch and accelerator pedals. Do not steer; give throttle only after the vehicle has settled onto the ground.
4) Brake only with the foot brake and the engine.
5) Avoid steering as far as possible, apart from small corrections. If steering here is done only by releasing the support brake, take care that in a left turn the RIGHT steering lever, and in a right turn the LEFT steering lever, is pulled, because here the engine is decelerating the tracks (steering reversal).
c) Stopping and holding the vehicle on a gradient: facing uphill
1) Pull both steering levers, disengage the main clutch.
2) Depress the foot-brake pedal fully and lock it.
3) Engage first gear.

Original page 68
31. Befahren von Steilhaengen (Fortsetzung)  -  31. Negotiating steep slopes (continued)
4) Switch off the engine.
5) Place blocks, stones or the like behind the track.
d) Stopping and securing the vehicle on a gradient: facing downhill
1) Brake with the foot brake, disengage the main clutch and lock the foot-brake pedal.
2) Engage first gear.
3) Switch off the engine.
4) Place blocks, stones or the like in front of the track.
e) Moving off on a gradient: facing uphill (foot-brake pedal locked, 1st gear engaged)
1) Remove the chocks or the like.
2) Disengage the clutch and start the engine.
3) Engage the main clutch smoothly.
4) While the main clutch takes hold, slowly release the foot brake.
f) Moving off on a gradient: facing downhill (foot-brake pedal locked, 1st gear engaged)
1) Remove the chocks or the like.
2) Disengage the clutch and start the engine.
3) Engage the main clutch smoothly.
4) While the main clutch takes hold, slowly release the foot brake.
g) Stopping on level ground
1) Apply the foot brake gently and slowly, or pull both steering levers evenly.
2) If the engine is very hot, idle for a few minutes to cool down.
32. Ueberwinden von Hindernissen  -  32. Overcoming obstacles
Obstacles (ditches, remains of walls, tree trunks, etc.) should be taken in the low gears where possible. When driving in wooded terrain, care must be taken that the armoured hull (Panzerwanne) does not become hung up on tree trunks. If, however, the vehicle does become grounded on a tree ...

Original page 69
32. Ueberwinden von Hindernissen (Fortsetzung)  -  32. Overcoming obstacles (continued)
... trunk, it can be pulled down again with the aid of the towing cable provided, which is attached to the track and to a fixed object (tree or the like).
When pushing over trees, drive in such a way that the vehicle is not lifted by the root system of a falling tree. The final push on the falling tree must be applied by driving up onto it with one track.
In very poor terrain (undulations and holes), drive in such a way that excessively heavy shocks and stresses are avoided.
